schema.name: TZPID source truth generated from canonical equation master

identity.canonical_title: Torus Curvature Ratio: in Differential Geometry, Consider a Torus (Doughnut Shape)…

theory.canonical_statement: • Torus Curvature Ratio: In differential geometry, consider a torus (doughnut shape) defined by a major radius $R$ (from center of hole to center of tube) and minor radius $r$ (tube radius). The Gaussian curvature on the outer equator $($\backslash phi=0$)$ is positive $$K_{\backslash text{outer}}=1/[R(R+r)]$,$ whereas on the inner equator ( $($\backslash phi=\backslash pi$)$ it is negative $K_{\text{inner}}=-1/[R(R-r)]$. The ratio of magnitudes is $SUfrac{|K_{\backslash text{inner}}|{K_{\backslash text{outer}}=\backslash frac{R+r}{,R-r,}S$. If we set $\frac{R}{r}\approx2.46$, this curvature ratio becomes $\approx2.37037$, which is 2x (two times 1.185185). In other words, a torus whose major radius is about 2.46 times its minor radius has an inner/outer curvature contrast of 237.037%, exactly double the 118.5% factor. This is a striking geometrical realization of the number: $\chi \approx1.185$ emerges as a "threshold" ratio distinguishing a moderately thick torus from a near-spherical
theory.technical_interpretation: Torus Curvature Ratio: in Differential Geometry, Consider a Torus (Doughnut Shape)… is a scaling / order-of-magnitude relation, written K_backslash textouter=1/[R(R+r)], whereas on the inner equator ( (backslash phi=. It expresses a scaling or proportionality, capturing how the quantity grows with its parameters. It is built from χ, R, r, w, h, a, defining K_backslash textouter. Within the Trawin Zero Point registry it serves as one element of the framework's mathematical narrative.

latex_blocks: K_{\backslash text{outer}}=1/[R(R+r)]$,$ whereas on the inner equator ( $($\backslash phi=\backslash pi$)$ it is negative $K_{\text{inner}}=-1/[R(R-r)]$. The ratio of magnitudes is $SUfrac{|K_{\backslash text{inner}}|{K_{\backslash text{outer}}=\backslash frac{R+r}{,R-r,}S$. If we set $\frac{R}{r}\approx2.46$, this curvature ratio becomes $\approx2.37037$, which is 2x (two times 1.185185). In other words, a torus whose major radius is about 2.46 times its minor radius has an inner/outer curvature contrast of 237.037%, exactly double the 118.5% factor. This is a striking geometrical realization of the number: $\chi \approx1.185$ emerges as a "threshold" ratio distinguishing a moderately thick torus from a near-spherical torus. (A very thin torus gives a curvature ratio near 1, while 2.37 marks a transition to a much more pronounced donut shape.) • Wave Modes and Half-Phases: The value 1.185 appears tantalizingly close to certain wave phenomena benchmarks. For example, the first zero of the Bessel function $J_0(x)$ occurs at $x\approx2.4048$, which is important in resonance of circular membranes and waveguides. Half of that value is
